---
title: "Emotive Rhetoric in the House of Commons Before and After the Brexit Referendum: A Dictionary-Based Analysis of Parliamentary Debate"
author: "Zhitong Chen"
date: "2026-04-20"
output: word_document
---

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# load all required packages
library(tidyverse)          # core data wrangling and ggplot2 visualisation
library(data.table)         # fast reading of large csv files
library(tidytext)           # tidy text mining framework (Silge and Robinson 2017)
library(quanteda)           # quantitative text analysis infrastructure
library(quanteda.textstats) # readability scores and text-level statistics
library(scales)             # improved axis label formatting for ggplot2
library(textdata)           # provides access to sentiment lexicons (e.g. Bing)

# set a temporary directory for package downloads (specific to my laptop)
Sys.setenv(TMPDIR = "C:/Temp")
dir.create("C:/Temp", showWarnings = FALSE)
```

```{r load-data, cache=FALSE}
# read UK parliamentary speech data from Osnabrugge et al. (2021)
# fread() from data.table is used for speed on this large csv file
data <- fread("uk_data.csv", encoding = "UTF-8")

# convert the date column from character to Date class
# required for all subsequent time-based filtering and grouping
data$date <- as.Date(data$date)
```

```{r inspect-data}
# verify successful data loading with basic summary statistics
cat("Total speeches:", nrow(data), "\n")
cat("Variables:", ncol(data), "\n")

# confirm the temporal coverage of the datase
cat("Date range:", as.character(min(data$date, na.rm = TRUE)), "to", 
    as.character(max(data$date, na.rm = TRUE)), "\n")
```

```{r define-period}
# define the Brexit referendum date as the analytical cutpoint
brexit_date <- as.Date("2016-06-23") # referendum took place on 23 June 2016

# create a binary indicator for the post-referendum period
# post_brexit = 1 for speeches on or after referendum day, 0 otherwise
# period is a labelled version of the same variable for use in plots
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    post_brexit = ifelse(date >= brexit_date, 1, 0),         # binary indicator
    period = ifelse(date >= brexit_date, "Post-Referendum",    "Pre-Referendum")                                          # labelled factor
  )
# verify the split between pre- and post-referendum speeches
cat("Full data pre-referendum:", sum(data$post_brexit == 0), "\n")
cat("Full data post-referendum:", sum(data$post_brexit == 1), "\n")
```

```{r stratified-sample}
# draw a stratified random sample balanced across four cells:
# government x post_brexit (2 x 2 = 4 groups, 12500 speeches each)
# the seed is fixed at 12345 so the sample is fully reproducible
set.seed(12345)

data_samp <- data %>%                   
  group_by(government, post_brexit) %>% # stratify by the two key variables
  slice_sample(n = 12500) %>%           # draw 12500 random rows per cell
  ungroup()                             # remove grouping for later operations

# confirm sample size and verify equal cell sizes
cat("Sample size:", nrow(data_samp), "\n")
table(data_samp$government, data_samp$post_brexit)
```

```{r create-variables}
# create time-related variables on the sampled data for trend analysis
data_samp$year <- as.numeric(format(data_samp$date, "%Y"))   # calendar year
data_samp$month <- as.numeric(format(data_samp$date, "%m"))  # month number
data_samp$year_month <- format(data_samp$date, "%Y-%m")      # year-month label

# create labelled categorical variables for plotting and tables
data_samp <- data_samp %>%
  mutate(
    period = ifelse(date >= brexit_date,                    # period label
                    "Post-Referendum", "Pre-Referendum"),
    side = ifelse(government == 1, "Government", "Opposition") # side label
  )
```

```{r debate-type}
# classify each speech into a debate type category
# used as a control variable in Model 3 to test H3
# case_when assigns the most specific category first
# speeches not matching any special type default to "Regular Debate"
data_samp <- data_samp %>%
  mutate(debate_type = case_when(
    pm_questions == 1 ~ "PMQs",                         # Prime Minister's Questions
    m_questions == 1 ~ "Other Question Time",           # other ministerial questions
    u_questions == 1 ~ "Urgent Questions",              # urgent questions
    queen_debate_day1 == 1 ~ "Queen's Speech Opening",  # Queen's Speech day 1
    queen_debate_others == 1 ~ "Queen's Speech Other",  # Queen's Speech other days
    TRUE ~ "Regular Debate"                             # all remaining speeches
  ))
```

```{r load-dictionaries}
# load the domain-specific dictionaries from Osnabrugge et al. (2021)
# emotive_uk.csv: 2015 words classified as emotive in parliamentary speech
# neutral_uk.csv: 2095 words classified as neutral in parliamentary speech
emotive_words <- read.csv("emotive_uk.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
neutral_words <- read.csv("neutral_uk.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# trim whitespace from word columns to prevent silent matching failures
emotive_words$word <- trimws(emotive_words$word)
neutral_words$word <- trimws(neutral_words$word)

# confirm the size of each dictionary
cat("Emotive dictionary size:", nrow(emotive_words), "words\n")
cat("Neutral dictionary size:", nrow(neutral_words), "words\n")
```

```{r tokenize}
# tokenise all speech texts into one-word-per-row (tidy text) format
# unnest_tokens converts to lowercase automatically; stemming is not applied
# stop words are retained because they may overlap with dictionary entries
tidy_speeches <- data_samp %>%
  select(id_speech, text, government, date, year, year_month,
         post_brexit, period, side, debate_type) %>% # keep only needed columns
  unnest_tokens(word, text)                          # one token per row individual words

# count total words in each speech for use as the denominato
words_per_speech <- tidy_speeches %>%  
  group_by(id_speech) %>%       # group by speech identifier
  summarise(total_words = n())  # count tokens per speech

# summary statistics on token counts
cat("Total tokens:", nrow(tidy_speeches), "\n")
cat("Mean words per speech:", round(mean(words_per_speech$total_words), 1), "\n")
```

```{r compute-score}
# count emotive dictionary words per speech via inner_join
# inner_join keeps only tokens found in the emotive word list 
emotive_counts <- tidy_speeches %>%
  inner_join(emotive_words, by = "word") %>%  # match tokens to emotive list
  group_by(id_speech) %>%                     # group by speech
  summarise(n_emotive = n())                  # count matched emotive tokens

# count neutral dictionary words per speech using the same logic
neutral_counts <- tidy_speeches %>%           
  inner_join(neutral_words, by = "word") %>%  # match tokens to neutral list
  group_by(id_speech) %>%                     # group by speech
  summarise(n_neutral = n())                  # count matched neutral words

# combine counts with total words and compute the emotive rhetoric score
# score = (pct emotive) - (pct neutral), following Osnabrugge et al. (2021)
speech_scores <- words_per_speech %>%
  left_join(emotive_counts, by = "id_speech") %>%   # add emotive counts
  left_join(neutral_counts, by = "id_speech") %>%   # add neutral counts
  mutate(
    n_emotive = replace_na(n_emotive, 0),           # zero if no emotive matches
    n_neutral = replace_na(n_neutral, 0),           # zero if no neutral matches
    pct_emotive = (n_emotive / total_words) * 100,  # emotive words as pct of total
    pct_neutral = (n_neutral / total_words) * 100,  # neutral words as pct of total
    own_score = pct_emotive - pct_neutral           # emotive rhetoric score
  )

# merge scores into main dataframe
data_samp <- data_samp %>%
  left_join(speech_scores %>% select(id_speech, own_score, 
                                      pct_emotive, pct_neutral,
                                      total_words),
            by = "id_speech")

# summary statistics for the computed emotive rhetoric score
summary(data_samp$own_score)
```

```{r validate-score}
# validate computed score against the authors' pre-calculated variable
# high correlation confirms our implementation replicates theirs
cor_val <- cor(data_samp$own_score, data_samp$emotive_rhetoric,
               use = "complete.obs")
cat("Correlation with original authors' score:", round(cor_val, 4), "\n")
```


```{r fig1-yearly-trend, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
# Figure 1: yearly trend in emotive rhetoric score with referendum marker
# shows the mean own_score by calendar year with 95% confidence intervals

# compute yearly mean and standard error
yearly_trend <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(year) %>%                               # group speeches by year
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),    # mean score per year
    se = sd(own_score, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),  # standard error of the mean
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# plot the yearly trend as a line with confidence ribbon
ggplot(yearly_trend, aes(x = year, y = mean_score)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +        # trend line
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue", size = 2) +              # annual data points
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = mean_score - 1.96 * se,            # lower 95% CI
                  ymax = mean_score + 1.96 * se),           # upper 95% CI
              alpha = 0.2, fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2016.5, linetype = "dashed",      # vertical referendum line
             colour = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = 2016.7, y = max(yearly_trend$mean_score),
           label = "Referendum", hjust = 0, colour = "red",  # label for referendum
           size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Mean Emotive Rhetoric Score") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2002, 2019, by = 2)) +     # x-axis tick marks
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r pre-post-test}
# formal comparison of emotive rhetoric before and after the referendum
# compute group means, standard deviations and 95% confidence intervals
period_comparison <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),  # group mean
    sd_score = sd(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),      # standard deviation
    n = n(),                                     # group size
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    se = sd_score / sqrt(n),                    # standard error
    ci_lower = mean_score - 1.96 * se,          # lower 95% CI
    ci_upper = mean_score + 1.96 * se           # upper 95% CI
  )

period_comparison

# two-sample t-test comparing pre- and post-referendum scores
t_result <- t.test(own_score ~ post_brexit, data = data_samp)
cat("t-statistic:", round(t_result$statistic, 3), "\n")
cat("p-value:", format.pval(t_result$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")

# Cohen's d as a standardised measure of effect size
# difference in means divided by the pooled standard deviation
pooled_sd <- sqrt(
  (period_comparison$sd_score[1]^2 + period_comparison$sd_score[2]^2) / 2
)
cohens_d <- abs(diff(period_comparison$mean_score)) / pooled_sd
cat("Cohen's d:", round(cohens_d, 4), "\n")
```

```{r fig2-pre-post-by-side, fig.width=7, fig.height=5}
# Figure 2: grouped bar chart comparing pre/post scores by government vs opposition
# tests H2 visually by showing whether the increase differs by side of the House

# compute group means and confidence intervals by period and side
period_by_side <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period, side) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),   # group mean
    sd_score = sd(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),       # group standard deviation
    n = n(),                                      # group size
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    se = sd_score / sqrt(n),                       # standard error
    ci_lower = mean_score - 1.96 * se,             # lower CI bound
    ci_upper = mean_score + 1.96 * se              # upper CI bound
  )

period_by_side

# create grouped bar chart
ggplot(period_by_side,
       aes(x = period, y = mean_score, fill = side)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity",
           position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +  # side-by-side bars
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper),              # error bars
                position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.15) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Mean Emotive Rhetoric Score", fill = "") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Government" = "steelblue",          # colour scheme
                                "Opposition" = "indianred")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r regression-models}
# three nested OLS regression models testing H1, H2 and H3
# Model 1: post_brexit only (tests H1)
model1 <- lm(own_score ~ post_brexit, data = data_samp)

# Model 2: adds government and its interaction with post_brexit (tests H2)
# a significant interaction would indicate asymmetric effects
model2 <- lm(own_score ~ post_brexit * government, data = data_samp)

# Model 3: adds debate type dummies as controls (tests H3)
# significant post_brexit here means the effect is not an artefact of debate mix
model3 <- lm(own_score ~ post_brexit * government + debate_type, 
             data = data_samp)

# print full summary for each model
cat("=== Model 1: Brexit effect only ===\n")
summary(model1)

cat("\n=== Model 2: Brexit x Government interaction ===\n")
summary(model2)

cat("\n=== Model 3: With debate type controls ===\n")
summary(model3)
```

```{r fig3-yearly-by-side, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
# Figure 3: yearly trend split by government vs opposition
# this visualises whether the two sides diverge after the referendum

# compute yearly means separately for government and opposition
yearly_by_side <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(year, side) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),    # mean per year per side
    se = sd(own_score, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),  # standard error
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# plot two trend lines with referendum marker
ggplot(yearly_by_side, aes(x = year, y = mean_score, colour = side)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   # trend lines
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +        # data points
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2016.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = 2016.7, y = max(yearly_by_side$mean_score),
           label = "Referendum", hjust = 0, colour = "grey40", size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Mean Emotive Rhetoric Score", colour = "") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Government" = "steelblue",
                                  "Opposition" = "indianred")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2002, 2019, by = 2)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r fig4-quarterly, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
# Figure 4: quarterly trend zoomed in on the 2014-2019 period
# provides a closer view of the trajectory around the referendum date

# compute quarterly means for speeches from 2014 onward
quarterly <- data_samp %>%
  filter(year >= 2014) %>%                                      # restrict to 2014+
  mutate(quarter = paste0(year, "-Q", ceiling(month / 3))) %>%     # create quarter label
  group_by(quarter) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),                   # quarterly mean
    se = sd(own_score, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),                 # standard error
    mid_date = median(date),                                      # midpoint date for x-axis
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# plot quarterly trend with referendum marker and confidence ribbon
ggplot(quarterly, aes(x = mid_date, y = mean_score)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = mean_score - 1.96 * se,
                  ymax = mean_score + 1.96 * se),
              alpha = 0.2, fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = brexit_date,              # vertical referendum line
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = brexit_date + 30,
           y = max(quarterly$mean_score),
           label = "23 June 2016", hjust = 0, colour = "red", size = 3.5) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Mean Emotive Rhetoric Score") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%Y-%m", date_breaks = "6 months") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r word-shift}
# analyse which emotive dictionary words shifted most after the referendum
# this helps interpret the substantive nature of the aggregate change

# count total tokens in each period for computing rates
total_pre <- tidy_speeches %>% filter(post_brexit == 0) %>% nrow()   # total pre-ref tokens
total_post <- tidy_speeches %>% filter(post_brexit == 1) %>% nrow()  # total post-ref tokens

# compute per-10,000-word rate for each emotive word in the pre-referendum period
emotive_pre <- tidy_speeches %>%
  filter(post_brexit == 0) %>%                     # select pre-referendum tokens
  inner_join(emotive_words, by = "word") %>%       # match against emotive dictionary
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%                     # count occurrences of each word
  mutate(rate_pre = (n / total_pre) * 10000) %>%   # normalise to rate per 10,000
  select(word, rate_pre)

# same computation for the post-referendum period
emotive_post <- tidy_speeches %>%
  filter(post_brexit == 1) %>%                       # select post-referendum tokens
  inner_join(emotive_words, by = "word") %>%         # match against emotive dictionary
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%                       # count occurrences
  mutate(rate_post = (n / total_post) * 10000) %>%   # normalise to rate per 10,000
  select(word, rate_post)

# join pre and post rates and compute the change for each word
word_shift <- emotive_pre %>%
  inner_join(emotive_post, by = "word") %>%    # merge pre and post rates
  mutate(change = rate_post - rate_pre) %>%    # difference in rates
  arrange(desc(change))                        # sort by largest increase

cat("Top 15 emotive words that increased most after the referendum:\n")
head(word_shift, 15)

cat("\nTop 15 emotive words that decreased most after the referendum:\n")
tail(word_shift, 15)
```

```{r fig5-word-shift, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
# Figure 5: bar chart of the top 10 words that increased and decreased most

# select the top 10 increases and top 10 decreases
top_increase <- word_shift %>% head(10) %>% mutate(direction = "Increased")
top_decrease <- word_shift %>% tail(10) %>% mutate(direction = "Decreased")
shift_plot_data <- bind_rows(top_increase, top_decrease)   # combine for plotting

# horizontal bar chart ordered by change magnitude
ggplot(shift_plot_data, aes(x = reorder(word, change), y = change,
                             fill = direction)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7) +     # bars
  coord_flip() +                                  # horizontal layout
  labs(x = "", y = "Change in Rate (per 10,000 words)", fill = "") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Increased" = "indianred",
                                "Decreased" = "steelblue")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```
```{r readability-analysis}
# Supplementary Analysis: linguistic complexity
# compute Flesch-Kincaid readability score for each speech
# higher scores  indicate easier (less complex) language
# this complements the emotive rhetoric measure by examining
# whether the post-referendum period also saw a change in complexity

# create a quanteda corpus object from the sampled speeches
corp_samp <- corpus(data_samp, text_field = "text")

# compute Flesch-Kincaid grade level for each speech
# textstat_readability is from the quanteda.textstats package 
readability_scores <- textstat_readability(corp_samp, 
                                            measure = "Flesch.Kincaid")

# attach readability scores back to the main dataframe
data_samp$readability <- readability_scores$Flesch.Kincaid

# compare mean readability before and after the referendum
readability_comparison <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_readability = mean(readability, na.rm = TRUE),  # mean complexity score
    sd_readability = sd(readability, na.rm = TRUE),      # standard deviation
    n = n(),                                              # group size
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Readability (Flesch-Kincaid) by period:\n")
readability_comparison

# t-test for readability difference
t_read <- t.test(readability ~ post_brexit, data = data_samp)
cat("Readability t-statistic:", round(t_read$statistic, 3), "\n")
cat("Readability p-value:", format.pval(t_read$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")
```

```{r robustness-original}
# Robustness Check 1: replicate using the authors' pre-computed score
# this verifies that our findings do not depend on our tokenisation
original_comparison <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    own_dict = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),          # my computed score
    original = mean(emotive_rhetoric, na.rm = TRUE),   # authors' pre-computed score 
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Comparison of our score vs original authors' score:\n")
original_comparison
```

```{r robustness-bing}
# Robustness Check 2: compare with the Bing general-purpose sentiment lexicon
# Bing measures positive/negative sentiment, a different construct from
# domain-specific emotive rhetoric; this tests sensitivity to dictionary choice

# count positive and negative Bing words per speech

bing_scores <- tidy_speeches %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") %>%  # match tokens against Bing
  count(id_speech, sentiment) %>%                # count pos and neg per speech
  pivot_wider(names_from = sentiment,            # reshape to one row per speech
              values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  left_join(words_per_speech, by = "id_speech") %>%   # add total word count
  mutate(bing_net = ((positive - negative) /
                       total_words) * 100)

# merge Bing scores into the main dataframe
data_samp <- data_samp %>%
  left_join(bing_scores %>% select(id_speech, bing_net), by = "id_speech")

# compare Bing sentiment by period
bing_comparison <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    bing_sentiment = mean(bing_net, na.rm = TRUE),  # mean Bing score per period
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Bing sentiment by period:\n")
bing_comparison
```

```{r robustness-correlation}
# Robustness Check 3: correlation between the three sentiment measures
# low correlation between domain-specific and general-purpose measures
# confirms they capture different aspects of language
cor_table <- data_samp %>%
  select(own_score, emotive_rhetoric, bing_net) %>%  # select the three scores
  drop_na() %>%                                     # remove missing values
  cor()                                             # compute correlation matrix

cat("Correlation matrix:\n")
round(cor_table, 3)
```

```{r robustness-exclude-short}
# Robustness Check 4: exclude very short speeches (fewer than 20 words)
# short speeches may produce extreme percentage scores due to small denominators
data_long <- data_samp %>% filter(total_words >= 20)  # keep only speeches >= 20 words

# compare means on the restricted sample
long_comparison <- data_long %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),         # mean score per period
    n = n(),                                             # sample size per period
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Results excluding speeches shorter than 20 words:\n")
long_comparison
```

```{r}
# Robustness Check 5: remove formulaic words from the emotive dictionary
# "thank" and "house" are the two largest movers on the increase list
# but both function as parliamentary conventions in most contexts
# this test checks whether the main finding survives their exclusion

# create a filtered emotive dictionary without these two words
emotive_filtered <- emotive_words %>%
  filter(!word %in% c("thank", "house"))                  # drop two formulaic items
cat("Filtered dictionary size:", nrow(emotive_filtered), "words\n")

# recount emotive words per speech using the filtered dictionary
emotive_counts_filtered <- tidy_speeches %>%
  inner_join(emotive_filtered, by = "word") %>%            # match against filtered list
  group_by(id_speech) %>%                                  # group by speech
  summarise(n_emotive_filt = n())                          # count filtered matches

# recompute the emotive rhetoric score with the filtered dictionary
score_filtered <- words_per_speech %>%
  left_join(emotive_counts_filtered, by = "id_speech") %>% # add filtered emotive counts
  left_join(neutral_counts, by = "id_speech") %>%          # add neutral counts as before
  mutate(
    n_emotive_filt = replace_na(n_emotive_filt, 0),        # zero if no matches
    n_neutral = replace_na(n_neutral, 0),                  # zero if no matches
    score_filtered = (n_emotive_filt / total_words) * 100  # filtered emotive pct
                     - (n_neutral / total_words) * 100     # minus neutral pct
  )

# merge the filtered score into the main dataframe
data_samp <- data_samp %>%
  left_join(score_filtered %>%
              select(id_speech, score_filtered),
            by = "id_speech")

# compare original and filtered scores by period
filter_comparison <- data_samp %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  summarise(
    original_score = mean(own_score, na.rm = TRUE),        # original dictionary score
    filtered_score = mean(score_filtered, na.rm = TRUE),   # score without thank/house
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Original vs filtered dictionary scores by period:\n")
filter_comparison

# t-test on filtered score
t_filtered <- t.test(score_filtered ~ post_brexit, data = data_samp)
cat("Filtered score t-statistic:", round(t_filtered$statistic, 3), "\n")
cat("Filtered score p-value:", format.pval(t_filtered$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")
```
